text:
Admission Date:  2005-02-18              Discharge Date:   1993-05-14

Date of Birth:  1992-12-30             Sex:   M

Service: MEDICINE

Allergies:
No Known Allergies / Adverse Drug Reactions

Attending:Donna
Chief Complaint:
Bilateral Arm Pain and Nausea

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
1996-02-06 Percutaneous Coronary Intervention
1996-05-05 Cardiac Catheterization


History of Present Illness:
Mr. Reynolds is a 60y/o gentleman with pulmonary silicosis, DM2,
HTN, and GERD who presented to an OSH with nausea, arm and back
pain and was transferred here for inferior STEMI.

The patient notes that he first started experiencing arm pain
and nausea approxomately 3-4 days ago. He intially attributed
these symptoms to the steroids he had been recently started on
pulmonary silicosis, however, his sx became progressive worse.
The patient noted nausea and back and arm pain bilaterally which
was not alleviated with rest or exacerbated by exertion. The
patient reports no chest pain. This pain was non-positional and
not associated with jaw pain or diaphoresis.

On Thursday 1995-06-15, he continued to have severe nausea without
vomiting and back and arm pain and his wife urged him to go to
the emergency department. On the night prior to admission he
presented to East Texas Medical Center Jacksonville with an inferior STEMI: EKG had STE
in II, III, aVF, and V4-6. Labs were notable for TropI 1.0, CK
467, MB 67, BUN/creat 42/1.7, Plt 92, plasma glucose 451, urine
glucose >1000, B-OH 0.29. He was started on full dose ASA,
plavix load 600mg, and heparin gtt, and was transferred here for
emergent cardiac catheterization.
Here, cardiac catheterization revealed a totally occluded
mid-RCA culprit lesion. Thrombectomy was performed and 4 DES
(PROMUS) were placed in the mid-distal RCA with good
angiographic results. He was chest pain-free at the end of the
PCI, and is transferred to Wernersville State Hospital for further management.

At 0100 on 01-01 on the cardiology floor service, the patient
began to experience vague abdominal pain. An EKG obtained at
that time was unremarkable and he was given some maalox. At 430
the pain had gotten worse and was in his arms as well as his
back and he felt it was smiliar to his original presenting
symptoms. An EKG at that time showed significant elevations in
II,III, aVF. He was given Nitro with BP drop from 120 to 90.
Also was given morphine with minimal improvement in his pain.
Cardiology fellow was contactRichard Kaiser and he was taken to the cath lab
for urgent STEMI.
.
In the cath lab he was found to have instent thrombosis of the
RCA. A wire was passed and thrombus was removed, with subsequent
baloon dilation and in situ TPA injected. He was left with open
stents with poor distal flow (TIMI 2). He was on integrillin x24
hours and was chest pain free.  Echo on 1988-02-12 demonstrated
that compared with the prior study (images reviewed) of
2005-02-18, the function of the inferolateral segments and right
ventricular free wall has improved.
.
In the CCU, the patient received atorvastatin 80mg qd, aspirin
325mg qd, lisinopril 10mg qd (changed from captopril after
hemodynamic stability documented), metoprolol tartrate
uptitrated to 75mg tid, prasugrel 10mg qd.  The patient's
prednisone was titrated down to 10mg, then to 5mg on 1984-04-03.
The patient had hyperglycemia that was treated with insulin.

Past Medical History:
- Pulmonary silicosis
- DM2
- HTN
- GERD


Social History:
- Home: Lives with wife
- Occupation: Retired stone cutter
- Tobacco history: no
- ETOH: No
- Illicit drugs: No


Family History:
- No family history of early MI, arrhythmia, cardiomyopathies,
or sudden cardiac death; otherwise non-contributory.

Studies:
CARDIAC CATH (1996-02-06):
1. Selective coronary angiography of this co-dominant system
demonstrated severe single vessel CAD. The LMCA was a patent,
short
vessel. The LAD had was a large wrap-around vessel with mild
luminal
irregularities throughout. It gave rise to a major bifurcating
diagonal
branch, which had mild luminal irregularities and a 60% lesion
in the
ostium of the lower lobe of the vessel. There was slow flow
noted
throughout the LAD system. The LCX was a large vessel, which
took off
from the LMCA at a 90 degree angle. It was tortuous proximally,
and gave
rise to a very large tortuous OM2, that extended out to the
apex, and a
large bifurcating OM3. The AV groove LCX was atretic. Slow flow
was
noted throughout the OM system. The RCA had mild luminal
irregularities
proximally, followed by thrombotic occlusion with extensive
thrombus
burden filling the whole vessel. No significant collateral flow
was
noted from the L to the R system.
2. Limited resting hemodynamics revealed normal systemic
arterial
pressures with a measured central aortic pressure of 104/75/81.
3. Left ventriculography was deferred.
4. Successful Export thrombectomy of organized lamellar thrombus
in
proximally occluded RCA. Flow returned in vessel with overall
poor
distal run-off due to clot burden and cut-offs in distal
branches.
Successful PTCA using 3.0 mm balloon and 4 overlapping drug
eluting
stents from proximal to distal vessel. (Promus 3.0 x 24 mm
distally,
Promus 3.0 x 24 mm more proximally, Promus 4.0 x 28 mm more
proximally,
and Promus 4.0 x 16 mm in proximal RCA). Post-dilated to 4.0 mm.

5. Final angiography showing 10% residual stenosis in 3rd stent,
with
TIMI 2 flow in the vessel and distal edge dissection resolved in
final
(covered with the 4th Promus stent). Poor outflow related to
downstream
thrombus.

6. Hemostasis at right radial arterial access site using TR
band.
FINAL DIAGNOSIS:
1. Severe single vessel CAD with thrombotic occlusion of the
RCA.
2. Slow flow in the left coronary system.
3. Export thrombectomy of thrombotic occlusion in proximal RCA.
Very
significant clot burden throughout vessel.
4. PCI of proximal to distal RCA using 4 Promus Element DES.
5. Intracoronary tPA administered.
6. Wiring and 2.0 mm balloon dilatation in PDA in attempt to
improve
distal run-off.
7. Recommend the following medical regimen: a) integrillin gtt
x18h
post-PCI, b) heparin gtt x8h once TR band off, c) transition to
Prasugrel 60mg LD f/b 10mg/d starting on 2005-02-18, d) ASA 325mg/d
x3mo
f/b 162mg/d, e) global CV risk reduction

ECHO (2005-02-18):
The left atrium is mildly dilated. There is mild symmetric left
ventricular hypertrophy with normal cavity size. There is mild
regional left ventricular systolic dysfunction with mild
dyskinesis of the basal inferior wall and hypokinesis of the
basal half of the inferolateral wall. The remaining segments
contract normally (LVEF = 40 %). The right ventricular cavity is
mildly dilated with mild global free wall hypokinesis. The
aortic valve leaflets (3) are mildly thickened but aortic
stenosis is not present. No aortic regurgitation is seen. The
mitral valve appears structurally normal with trivial mitral
regurgitation. There is no mitral valve prolapse. The estimated
pulmonary artery systolic pressure is normal. There is a
trivial/physiologic pericardial effusion.

IMPRESSION: Mild symmetric left ventricular hypertrrophy with
mild regional systolic function c/w CAD (proximal RCA
distribution with involvement of the RV free wall).

CARDIAC CATH (1996-05-05):
COMMENTS:
1. Subacute stent thrombosis with emergent coronary angiography
revealing a proximal occluded RCA. Significant thrombus burden.
2. Successful thrombectomy and PTCA restoring flow to the
vessel.
3. Intracoronary tPA bolus administered.
4. Flow normal to RV branches.
FINAL DIAGNOSIS:
1. Subacute stent thrombosis. Inferior ST elevation. Thrombotic
occlusion of proximal RCA.
2. Successful thrombectomy and PTCA with restoration of flow.
3. Transfer to CCU, continue Integrilin x 24 hours. Continue
Aspirin and
Prasugrel.

ECHO (1988-02-12):
The left atrium is mildly dilated. There is mild symmetric left
ventricular hypertrophy with normal cavity size. There is mild
regional left ventricular systolic dysfunction with mild
hypokinesis of the basal to mid inferolateral segments. Doppler
parameters are indeterminate for left ventricular diastolic
function. Right ventricular chamber size is normal. with
borderline normal free wall function. The aortic valve leaflets
(3) are mildly thickened but aortic stenosis is not present. No
aortic regurgitation is seen. The mitral valve leaflets are
structurally normal. Mild (1+) mitral regurgitation is seen. The
pulmonary artery systolic pressure could not be determined.
There is a trivial/physiologic pericardial effusion. Compared
with the prior study (images reviewed) of 2005-02-18, the
function of the inferolateral segments and right ventricular
free wall has improved.

Brief Hospital Course:
60 year old male with a past medical history of poorly
controlled DM2, HLD, HTN, interstitial lung disease (silicosis)
who was transferred from Foundations Behavioral Health to Foundations Behavioral Health after he was found to be
having a inferior STEMI. Upon arrival to Cornerstone Hospital Of Southwest Louisiana he was taken to
the cath lab where his RCA was totally occluded. Four Promus
stents were placed in the RCA. The patient was sent to the floor
where he suffered an instent thrombosis 24 hours after his
procedure. He was taken back to the cath lab and flow was
restored to his RCA. He was sent to the CCU for monitoring. He
recovered quickly and was transferred to the floor and
subsequently sent home after physical therapy clearance.

Inferior STEMI: RCA occlusion. The patient presented with ST
elevations in II, III, AVF and elevated troponins. He was taken
to cath lab where drug eluting stents were placed. The procedure
was successful and he was transferred to the floor on
integrillin and heparin as well as loaded on effient and
aspirin. He did well the first day on the floor and his enzymes
trended down. He was started on low dose metoprolol,
atrovastatin 80, lisinopril, effient 10, and aspirin 325.
Approximately 18 hours following the procedure the patient
reported sudden intense nausea and bilateral arm pain. EKG
showed STEMI, he was taken back to the cath lab where he was
found to have an thrombosed his new stent. Flow was returned
after several hours in the lab. He was sent to the CCU in stable
condition. TTE showed mild RV hypokensis and slightly depressed
LVEF at 45%. The patient had no complications from the MI. He
showed no signs of CHF or heart block. He remained in sinus. As
a result of his STEMI, we strongly suggested to the patient that
he not travel on planes for the  next few weeks until his
condition stabilized.
- ASA 325 mg PO daily
- effient 10mg qd
- Metoprolol XL 150 as outpatient
- Atorvastatin 80mg
- Lisinopril 5 mg qd

# Pulmonary silicosis: Patient said that his PCP started him on
a two week course of steroids for his breathing problems which
he stopped taking 4 days prior to admission. The patient was
slowly tapered off his steroids while in the hospital. By the
time of discharge, his steroids were stopped. Since the patient
was never a smoker, there is no clear indication for systemic
glucocorticoids in treatment of chronic silicosis (Am Rev Michael Browns. 2016;143(4 Pt 1):814). Per outside records patient has been
considered for possible lung transplantation at Jones Memorial Hospital. This is
unconfirmed.

# DIABETES: Patient not currently taking any outpatient
medications for his diabetes. He presented with blood sugars in
the 500s. He had polyuria, polydipsia, and blurry vision. This
was rapidly corrected with insulin. Of note the patient required
large amounts of insulin to achieve euglycemia (~75u qd while on
low dose steroids). His A1c was 9.5. Per records from Foundations Behavioral Health,
his A1c has been as high as 11 in the past. Williams saw the
patient and made formal recommendations which were lantus and
humalog sliding scale as outpatient as well as Foundations Behavioral Health metformin
500mg. The patient was instructed several times how to use
insulin. He has follow up at the Williams within 1-2 weeks.

#Chronic Kidney Disease: Creatinine stable at 1.2.

Medication Changes: START
-aspirin 325 mg Tablet

-lisinopril 5 mg

2. No long distance travel until cleared by Interventional
Cardiology

Prednisone 40 qd

Discharge Instructions:
Dear Mr. Reynolds,
You were admitted to the hospital because you had a heart
attack.  You underwent cardiac catheterization on 1996-02-06 and
had 4 stents placed in the Right Coronary Artery of your heart
at that time.  After a day or so, a blood clot formed within
your stents which caused another heart attack, so you underwent
a second cardiac catheterization to remove as much of that clot
as possible.

You have been started on some new medications to help protect
your heart.  Prasugrel is the most important medication which is
helping to keep your coronary stents open.  Please do not stop
this medication without first talking to your cardiologist.

Your blood sugars were very high during much of your hospital
stay.  This may be partially because of the steroids
(prednisone) that you had been taking for your pulmonary
silicosis but it is mainly related to pure diabetes.

The following changes were made to your medications:
- Please START Prasugrel 10mg daily.
*** Please DO NOT STOP this medication for any reason
whatsoever, unless approved by your cardiologist. If you miss
any doses of this medication, your stents could clot off again
and cause another heart attack.***
- Please START Aspirin 325 mg every day
- Please START Atorvastatin 80 mg every night
- Please START Omeprazole 40 mg every day
- Please START metoprolol succinate (Toprol XL) 150 mg daily
- Please START lisinopril 5 mg once daily
- Please START Metformin 500 mg twice daily
- Please START Insulin
 - Take long-acting LANTUS (GLARGINE) 35 units at NIGHT
 - Take the short-acting HUMALOG as directed with meals
- Keep a diary of your blood sugars. Check & record daily
morning fingersticks, as well as 2 hour post-mealfingersticks.
Get a diary or start a collection of papers that have 4 columns
- one for morning and one for 2 hours after each meal and write
the date on the left hand margin. This record will be CRUCIAL
for you to help your doctors to manage your diabetes
- Please STOP your prednisone and discuss restarting with your
lung doctors.

INSULIN FIXED DOSE:
  Breakfast: 6u Humalog
  Lunch:     8u Humalog
  Dinner:    8u Humalog
  Bedtime:   35u Lantus

Please do not travel on airplanes until your cardiologist tells
you it is ok to do so.
